1: Moses addresses the tribal heads (rāʾšê hammattôt) rather than the whole congregation — the vow laws concern personal obligations whose enforcement falls within family authority structures. The formal opening zeh haddāḇār ʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH — this is the word YHWH commanded — frames the following legislation as direct divine command reported through Moses.
2: The foundational vow principle: a man who makes a נֶדֶר (neder) — a positive promise to offer something to YHWH — or a שְׁבוּעָה (šəḇûʿâ) — an oath binding himself — must not yəḥallel dəḇārô — not profane/defile his word. The verb חָלַל (ḥālal) in the Piel means to treat as common what is sacred: one's spoken word to YHWH has a sacral quality that makes breaking it a form of profanation. The positive command: ʾăšer yēṣēʾ mippîw yaʿăśeh — whatever proceeds from his mouth he shall do.
3: The law pivots to women, introducing three distinct legal categories based on household status. First: a woman in her father's house (bêt ʾāḇîhā) in her youth (binəʿûrêhā). The phrase nāʾ ʾissār ʿal-nap̱šāh — she binds a pledge upon her soul — uses the distinctive vocabulary of this chapter: אִסָּר (ʾissār) from ʾāsar (to bind), denoting a self-binding verbal commitment. The neder is a positive promise; the ʾissār is a self-denial/restriction pledge.
4: If her father hears (wəšāmaʿ ʾāḇîhā) and says nothing (weheḥĕrîš lāh) — the legal silence that constitutes tacit approval — all her vows and pledges stand. הֶחֱרִישׁ (heḥĕrîš) is the technical term for legally meaningful silence throughout this chapter (vv4,5,7,8,12,14); it is not mere inaction but a defined legal choice between disallowance and ratification. Silence = ratification.
5: If her father hēnîʾ ʾôtāh — disallows/withholds approval from her — on the day he hears, none of her vows stand. The verb נוּא (nûʾ) — to disallow, restrain, refuse — appears primarily in this chapter (vv5,8,9,12 etc.) for the paternal/spousal veto. The reason YHWH gives for the release from the vow: wəYHWH yislaḥ lāh — YHWH will forgive her. The failed vow is not her fault; the one in authority who annulled it bears responsibility.
6: Second category: a woman who makes a vow or a rash/hasty pledge (miḇṭāʾ śəp̄ātêhā) — the phrase מִבְטָא שְׂפָתַיִם (miḇṭāʾ śəp̄ātayim) literally means the utterance of lips (from bāṭaʾ, to speak hastily); this suggests impetuous vows made without full deliberation — and then marries (ûl·ʾîš tihyeh). The transition of household authority from father to husband requires a fresh legal rule.
7: If her husband hears and says nothing on the day he hears, her vows stand and her binding pledges hold. The same silence-as-ratification principle transfers from the paternal to the spousal context. The consistent structure across both contexts establishes the legal principle: the authority figure's timely silence ratifies; timely speech can annul; belated annulment is too late.
8: If her husband disallows (yānîʾ ʾôtāh) on the day he hears, he annuls the vow. The verse includes both the vow (neder) and the rash utterance of her lips (miḇṭāʾ śəp̄ātêhā). The husband's authority to annul on the day of hearing — no later — preserves the woman from vow obligations she has already incurred while preventing retrospective manipulation of vow law.
9: Third category: the widow (ʾalmānâ) and the divorced woman (gərûšâ). Their vows stand unconditionally — no paternal or spousal authority exists to annul them. kol-ʾăšer ʾāsərâ ʿal-nap̱šāh yāqûm ʿālêhā — all that she bound upon herself shall stand against her. The language yāqûm ʿālêhā — it stands against her — emphasizes that she alone bears the full weight of her vow commitments. Full legal autonomy comes with full vow responsibility.
10: The married-while-making-vow scenario elaborates: a vow or oath made while in her husband's house (bêt ʾîšāh). The same legal structure applies — husband may ratify by silence or annul by timely speech — extended to cover commitments made during the marriage rather than before it.
11: If he heard and said nothing and did not disallow her (lōʾ hēnîʾ ʾôtāh), all her vows and pledges stand. The doubling — silence plus non-disallowance — reinforces that the husband must actively exercise his veto on the day of hearing; failure to do so is legally binding ratification, not a recoverable omission.
12: If he fully annulled them (hāpēr yāp̄ēr ʾôtām) on the day of hearing — the infinitive absolute emphasizes complete annulment — then whatever she spoke is null. The annulled obligation is specifically categorized as things concerning the soul/person (kol-môṣāʾ śəp̄ātêhā lənādrêhā ûlə ʾissər nap̱šāh); YHWH forgives the unfulfilled vow.
13: The summary principle: every vow (kol-neder) and every oath of self-denial (kol-šəḇûʿat ʾissār lənannôt nāp̱eš) — her husband may confirm (yəqîmennû) or annul (yəp̄ērennû). The verb קוּם (qûm) in the Hiphil = to establish, uphold; פָּרַר (pārar) = to break, annul. The binary is absolute: confirmation or annulment, no middle option.
14: The silence-ratification principle reaches its precise legal statement: if the husband says nothing day after day (min-yôm ʾel-yôm), he confirms all her vows by the very act of continued silence — he has ratified them. The phrase heqîm ʾôtām kî-heḥĕrîš lāh — he confirmed them because he was silent to her — establishes silence over time as cumulative ratification, creating legal commitment through inaction.
15: The critical limiting case: if the husband annuls the vows after hearing them (ʾaḥărê šomʿô), he bears her iniquity (wənāśāʾ ʾet-ʿăwōnāh). The verb נָשָׂא עָוֹן (nāśāʾ ʿāwōn) — to bear/carry iniquity — is the same phrase used for substitutionary bearing of guilt throughout the Torah (Leviticus 5:1,17; Numbers 10:17). Belated annulment transfers moral culpability from the woman (who acted in good faith) to the husband (who delayed improperly).
16: The concluding rubric: ʾēlleh haḥuqqîm ʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe — these are the statutes YHWH commanded Moses. The scope stated — between a man and his wife, and between a father and his daughter in her youth — precisely delimits the law's application to the two household-authority relationships addressed. The rubric marks the end of the vow-law unit as a discrete legislative section.
